examples.title: Basic Mouse and Keyboard Input Detection
examples.description: Demonstrates basic input device handling - detecting mouse position, clicks, and keyboard key presses using pygame library for computer graphics applications
examples.code:
import pygame
import sys

# Initialize pygame
pygame.init()

# Set up display
WIDTH, HEIGHT = 800, 600
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((WIDTH, HEIGHT))
pygame.display.set_caption('Input Devices - Mouse & Keyboard')

# Colors
WHITE = (255, 255, 255)
BLUE = (0, 0, 255)
RED = (255, 0, 0)
GREEN = (0, 255, 0)

# Font for displaying input info
font = pygame.font.Font(None, 24)

# Main loop
running = True
while running:
    screen.fill(WHITE)
    
    # Event handling
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        if event.type == pygame.QUIT:
            running = False
        
        # Mouse click detection
        if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN:
            print(f"Mouse Click: {event.button} at position {event.pos}")
        
        # Keyboard press detection
        if event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN:
            print(f"Key Pressed: {pygame.key.name(event.key)}")
    
    # Get real-time mouse position
    mouse_pos = pygame.mouse.get_pos()
    mouse_pressed = pygame.mouse.get_pressed()
    
    # Display input information
    info_text = [
        f"Mouse Position: {mouse_pos}",
        f"Left Button: {mouse_pressed[0]}",
        f"Right Button: {mouse_pressed[2]}",
        f"Press ESC to exit"
    ]
    
    for i, text in enumerate(info_text):
        text_surface = font.render(text, True, BLUE)
        screen.blit(text_surface, (10, 10 + i * 25))
    
    # Draw circle at mouse position
    pygame.draw.circle(screen, RED, mouse_pos, 20)
    
    # Check keyboard state
    keys = pygame.key.get_pressed()
    if keys[pygame.K_SPACE]:
        pygame.draw.rect(screen, GREEN, (350, 250, 100, 100))
    
    pygame.display.flip()

pygame.quit()
sys.exit()
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates fundamental input device handling in computer graphics. 1) pygame.init() initializes the graphics library. 2) pygame.event.get() processes system events including mouse clicks (MOUSEBUTTONDOWN) and keyboard presses (KEYDOWN). 3) pygame.mouse.get_pos() provides real-time mouse position tracking. 4) pygame.mouse.get_pressed() checks which mouse buttons are currently held down. 5) pygame.key.get_pressed() allows continuous keyboard state checking for smooth movement. These input methods are essential for interactive graphics applications.

examples.title: Interactive Drawing Application with Multiple Input Devices
examples.description: Creates a simple paint-like application demonstrating mouse drag input, keyboard shortcuts, and output to display - simulating graphics tablet functionality
examples.code:
import pygame
import sys

pygame.init()

# Display setup
WIDTH, HEIGHT = 900, 650
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((WIDTH, HEIGHT))
pygame.display.set_caption('Graphics I/O - Drawing Application')

# Colors
WHITE = (255, 255, 255)
BLACK = (0, 0, 0)
COLORS = [(255, 0, 0), (0, 255, 0), (0, 0, 255), (255, 255, 0), (255, 0, 255)]

# Drawing state
drawing = False
current_color = BLACK
brush_size = 5
last_pos = None

# Font
font = pygame.font.Font(None, 20)

def draw_toolbar():
    """Draw color palette and tools"""
    pygame.draw.rect(screen, (200, 200, 200), (0, 0, WIDTH, 40))
    
    # Color buttons
    for i, color in enumerate(COLORS):
        pygame.draw.circle(screen, color, (30 + i * 50, 20), 15)
    
    # Brush size indicator
    size_text = font.render(f"Brush: {brush_size}px", True, BLACK)
    screen.blit(size_text, (280, 12))
    
    # Instructions
    instr = font.render("1-5: Colors | +/-: Brush Size | C: Clear | S: Save", True, BLACK)
    screen.blit(instr, (450, 12))

def draw_output():
    """Simulate output device (display) refresh"""
    pygame.display.flip()

# Main loop
running = True
while running:
    screen.fill(WHITE)
    draw_toolbar()
    
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        if event.type == pygame.QUIT:
            running = False
        
        # Mouse input - drawing
        if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN:
            if event.pos[1] > 40:  # Below toolbar
                drawing = True
                last_pos = event.pos
        
        if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONUP:
            drawing = False
            last_pos = None
        
        # Mouse drag - continuous drawing
        if drawing and event.type == pygame.MOUSEMOTION:
            if last_pos:
                pygame.draw.line(screen, current_color, last_pos, event.pos, brush_size)
            last_pos = event.pos
        
        # Keyboard input - tool selection
        if event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN:
            # Color selection (keys 1-5)
            if event.key == pygame.K_1:
                current_color = COLORS[0]
            elif event.key == pygame.K_2:
                current_color = COLORS[1]
            elif event.key == pygame.K_3:
                current_color = COLORS[2]
            elif event.key == pygame.K_4:
                current_color = COLORS[3]
            elif event.key == pygame.K_5:
                current_color = COLORS[4]
            
            # Brush size control
            elif event.key == pygame.K_PLUS or event.key == pygame.K_EQUALS:
                brush_size = min(50, brush_size + 1)
            elif event.key == pygame.K_MINUS:
                brush_size = max(1, brush_size - 1)
            
            # Clear screen
            elif event.key == pygame.K_c:
                screen.fill(WHITE)
            
            # Save output (simulated)
            elif event.key == pygame.K_s:
                pygame.image.save(screen, "drawing_output.png")
                print("Image saved to drawing_output.png")
    
    # Real-time mouse position display
    if pygame.mouse.get_pos()[1] > 40:
        pos_text = font.render(f"Position: {pygame.mouse.get_pos()}", True, BLACK)
        screen.blit(pos_text, (10, 50))
    
    draw_output()

pygame.quit()
sys.exit()
examples.explanation: This advanced example simulates a graphics tablet and display output system. It demonstrates: 1) Mouse drag input (MOUSEMOTION) for continuous drawing like a graphics tablet. 2) Multiple input handling - both mouse and keyboard simultaneously. 3) Keyboard shortcuts (1-5 for colors, +/- for brush size) mimicking professional graphics tablets. 4) Output handling - pygame.display.flip() refreshes the display (output device). 5) File output - pygame.image.save() simulates saving to printer or file. The toolbar represents the GUI controls common in graphics applications. This exemplifies how input devices (mouse/keyboard) interact with output devices (monitor) in computer graphics.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of drawn pixels

examples.title: Light Pen Simulation and Display Device Concepts
examples.description: Simulates light pen input behavior (detecting screen positions) and demonstrates refresh rate concepts for CRT-style display output devices in computer graphics
examples.code:
import pygame
import time
import random

pygame.init()

# Display configuration (simulating CRT monitor)
WIDTH, HEIGHT = 800, 600
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((WIDTH, HEIGHT))
pygame.display.set_caption('Light Pen Simulation - CRT Display Concept')

# Colors
BLACK = (0, 0, 0)
GREEN = (0, 255, 0)
RED = (255, 0, 0)
WHITE = (255, 255, 255)

# Light pen state
light_pen_active = False
light_pen_pos = None
pen_sensitivity = 30  # Simulated light detection radius

# Frame rate control (simulating refresh rate)
clock = pygame.time.Clock()
REFRESH_RATE = 60  # Hz - typical CRT refresh rate

# Font
font = pygame.font.Font(None, 18)

class LightPen:
    """Simulates a light pen input device"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.position = (0, 0)
        self.triggered = False
    
    def detect(self, screen_surface, mouse_pos):
        """Simulate light pen detection - checks if pointing at bright area"""
        x, y = mouse_pos
        
        # Get pixel color at position
        if 0 <= x < WIDTH and 0 <= y < HEIGHT:
            color = screen_surface.get_at((x, y))
            # Light pen detects bright pixels (simulating photodiode)
            brightness = sum(color[:3]) / 3
            self.triggered = brightness > 100  # Threshold for detection
            self.position = mouse_pos
            return self.triggered
        return False
    
    def get_position(self):
        return self.position

def simulate_crt_refresh():
    """Simulates CRT refresh mechanism - raster scanning"""
    scanline_offset = (pygame.time.get_ticks() * 2) % HEIGHT
    return scanline_offset

def draw_scanlines():
    """Visualize CRT scanlines (output device characteristic)"""
    for y in range(0, HEIGHT, 3):
        pygame.draw.line(screen, (20, 20, 20), (0, y), (WIDTH, y))

# Initialize light pen
light_pen = LightPen()

# Grid for light pen target
target_pos = (random.randint(50, WIDTH-50), random.randint(50, HEIGHT-50))

# Main loop
running = True
frame_count = 0
fps_display = 60

print("Light Pen Simulation Started")
print("Move mouse to simulate light pen")
print("Click to 'trigger' the light pen")
print("Press ESC to exit")

while running:
    # Maintain constant refresh rate (CRT characteristic)
    clock.tick(REFRESH_RATE)
    frame_count += 1
    
    # Update FPS display every second
    if frame_count % 60 == 0:
        fps_display = clock.get_fps()
    
    screen.fill(BLACK)
    draw_scanlines()
    
    # Simulate CRT scanline animation
    scan_y = simulate_crt_refresh()
    pygame.draw.line(screen, GREEN, (0, scan_y), (WIDTH, scan_y), 2)
    
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        if event.type == pygame.QUIT:
            running = False
        
        # Simulate light pen button (mouse click)
        if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN:
            light_pen_active = True
            print(f"Light Pen Triggered at {event.pos}")
        
        if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONUP:
            light_pen_active = False
        
        if event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN:
            if event.key == pygame.K_ESCAPE:
                running = False
            # Move target randomly
            elif event.key == pygame.K_SPACE:
                target_pos = (random.randint(50, WIDTH-50), random.randint(50, HEIGHT-50))
    
    # Get mouse position (simulating light pen position)
    mouse_pos = pygame.mouse.get_pos()
    
    # Light pen detection simulation
    detected = light_pen.detect(screen, mouse_pos)
    
    # Draw target (simulated graphic element)
    pygame.draw.circle(screen, RED, target_pos, 20, 2)
    pygame.draw.circle(screen, RED, target_pos, 5)
    
    # Draw light pen position indicator
    if light_pen_active:
        color = GREEN if detected else WHITE
        pygame.draw.circle(screen, color, mouse_pos, pen_sensitivity, 2)
        pygame.draw.line(screen, color, (mouse_pos[0]-10, mouse_pos[1]), (mouse_pos[0]+10, mouse_pos[1]), 2)
        pygame.draw.line(screen, color, (mouse_pos[0], mouse_pos[1]-10), (mouse_pos[0], mouse_pos[1]+10), 2)
    
    # Display information
    info = [
        f"Refresh Rate: {REFRESH_RATE} Hz (CRT Simulation)",
        f"Actual FPS: {fps_display:.1f}",
        f"Light Pen: {'Active' if light_pen_active else 'Inactive'}",
        f"Position: {mouse_pos}",
        f"Detection: {'DETECTED' if detected else 'Not Detected'}",
        f"Target: {target_pos}",
        "Press SPACE to move target, ESC to exit"
    ]
    
    for i, text in enumerate(info):
        text_surf = font.render(text, True, GREEN)
        screen.blit(text_surf, (10, 10 + i * 18))
    
    pygame.display.flip()

pygame.quit()

print("\nCRT Light Pen Simulation Complete")
print("This demonstrates:")
print("1. Light Pen input device - detecting screen positions")
print("2. CRT display characteristics - refresh rate, scanlines")
print("3. Input-output device interaction in graphics systems")
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates historical input-output devices in computer graphics: 1) Light Pen - an early pointing device (1960s-80s) that detected light from CRT screen to determine position. The LightPen class simulates photodiode detection. 2) CRT Display - cathode ray tube monitors used raster scanning with refresh rates (60 Hz shown). The scanline visualization demonstrates this. 3) Input/Output Integration - shows how light pen (input) interacts with display (output) in a feedback loop. 4) Frame timing - clock.tick(60) simulates fixed refresh rates essential for graphics. This exemplifies how older graphics systems worked and helps students understand the evolution of I/O devices.
